---
title: "Supplementary Materials"
subtitle: "Children's Expectations of Emotional Intimacy in Close Relationships"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  docx:
    output-file: OMIT_supplement_rendered.docx
    number-sections: true
    reference-doc: apa-reference.docx
  pdf:
    output-file: OMIT_supplement.pdf
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    documentclass: article
    geometry: margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    mainfont: "Times New Roman"
    number-sections: true
    fig-pos: 'H'
    colorlinks: true
bibliography: references.bib
csl: apa.csl
fig-align: center
editor:
  markdown:
    wrap: 72
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
suppressMessages({ library(here); library(dplyr); library(readr); library(knitr) })

# All tables/figures come from 5. Analysis/code/OMIT_CLEAN.R (run it first).
res     <- here::here("5. Analysis", "results")
fig_dir <- here::here("5. Analysis", "figures")
diag_dir <- file.path(fig_dir, "diagnostics")

D <- read_csv(file.path(res, "model_diagnostics.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
M <- read_csv(file.path(res, "model_estimates.csv"),   show_col_types = FALSE)
B <- read_csv(file.path(res, "binomial_BF.csv"),       show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  mutate(cell = coalesce(relationship, measure))
CM <- read_csv(file.path(res, "circles_means.csv"),     show_col_types = FALSE)
CE <- read_csv(file.path(res, "circles_estimates.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
CA <- read_csv(file.path(res, "circles_age_estimates.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
SX <- read_csv(file.path(res, "gender_effects.csv"),    show_col_types = FALSE)

# helper: posterior median + 95% CI for a circles model term
# (model = study1_circles_child [default] or study1_circles_adult)
circ <- function(term, model = "study1_circles_child") {
  r <- dplyr::filter(CE, model == !!model, term == !!term)
  sprintf("median = %.2f, 95%% CI [%.2f, %.2f]", r$median, r$CI_low, r$CI_high)
}
# same, for the circles developmental (age) model
cage <- function(term) {
  r <- dplyr::filter(CA, term == !!term)
  sprintf("median = %.2f, 95%% CI [%.2f, %.2f]", r$median, r$CI_low, r$CI_high)
}

# pretty model labels
mod_lab <- c(
  study1_bf_child = "Study 1 — better friend (children)",     study1_bf_adult = "Study 1 — better friend (adults)",
  study1_food_child = "Study 1 — food sharing (children)",    study1_food_adult = "Study 1 — food sharing (adults)",
  study2_child = "Study 2 (children)", study2_adult = "Study 2 (adults)",
  study4A_child = "Study 4A (children)", study4A_adult = "Study 4A (adults)",
  study4B_child = "Study 4B (children)", study4B_adult = "Study 4B (adults)",
  study1_cellmeans_child = "Study 1 cell-means (children)",
  study1_cellmeans_adult = "Study 1 cell-means (adults)",
  study3A_child = "Study 3A (children)", study3A_adult = "Study 3A (adults)",
  study3B_child = "Study 3B (children)", study3B_adult = "Study 3B (adults)")

# full posterior table for a set of models (terms, median, 95% CI, ROPE, pd)
post_table <- function(models) {
  M |>
    filter(model %in% models) |>
    mutate(Model = recode(model, !!!mod_lab),
           Term  = gsub("^b_", "", term),
           `95% CI` = sprintf("[%.2f, %.2f]", CI_low, CI_high)) |>
    select(Model, Term, Median = median, `95% CI`, `% in ROPE` = pct_in_ROPE, pd)
}
```

> This document is reproducible: every table and figure is produced by
> `5. Analysis/code/OMIT_CLEAN.R` and read here via `here()`. Items
> marked **`[PLACEHOLDER]`** need author input.

# Supplementary Methods

## Participants

Full sample sizes and age/sex breakdowns are given in the main text.
Briefly, the Set-A studies (Studies 1, 3A, 4A) tested 57 children (6--9
years) and 47 adults; the Set-B/C studies (Studies 2, 3B, 4B) tested 105
children and 49 adults.

## Sample-size justification

For the Set-B/C studies we pre-registered a target of 56 children,
consistent with prior work that detected comparable effects [@woo2024].
However after we had tested 56 children we analyzed the data and the
results were inconclusive. Thus we decided to collect double the amount
which is allowed when using bayesian analyses [@wagenmakers2019five ]
Recruitment exceeded the target, yielding 105 children. Adult comparison
samples (47 and 49) were collected via an online platform. As is
standard for Bayesian mixed-effects models, sample size was justified by
analogy to prior effects rather than a frequentist power calculation
[see @vasishth2023sample].

## Exclusion criteria

Per the pre-registration, data were excluded for (a) consistent familial
interference (e.g., a caregiver or sibling biasing the child's
responses), (b) procedural error (deviation from the standard procedure
such that the participant did not see all vignettes/trials), or (c)
equipment failure (e.g., inadequate internet or device malfunction
preventing completion).

In the Set-A child sample, 2 of the 59 tested children were excluded
(final *N* = 57). One was excluded for a stimulus mishap during the
classmate trials (procedural error) and S043 because the child was
colorblind and could not complete the color-based response task. Three
further children were flagged for environmental issues (one zoom
interruption, two loud environments) but were retained. In the Set-B/C
child sample, 3 children excluded for refusing to answer any questions.
A small number of individual items were left blank or uncodable because
children did not answer questions, so the effective per-cell *N* is
103--104. The adult samples comprised 47 (Set A) and 49 (Set B/C)
complete responses.

# Analytic approach

Forced-choice responses were coded `chose_emotion` (1 = chose/was told
the emotion; 0 = the fact). For each cell we pre-registered and
conducted one-sided Bayesian binomial tests (JZS default prior) in the
pre-registered direction of the hypothesis (emotion \> chance for
Studies 1, 2, 4A, 3A, 3B; fact \> chance for Study 4B).

For the mixed-effects analyses we fit Bayesian probit generalized linear
mixed models with brms default priors. For children the model crossed
the condition factor(s) with mean-centered continuous age and included a
participant random intercept
(`chose_emotion ~ condition * age + (1 | ID)`); for adults, age was not
recorded, so the model was condition-only
(`chose_emotion ~ condition + (1 | ID)`).

**Inference criterion.** Following the pre-registration, an effect was
taken as credible when the 95% credible interval excluded 0; we
additionally report the percentage of the posterior within a region of
practical equivalence (ROPE) of \[−0.1, 0.1\] and the probability of
direction (pd).

# Model diagnostics

## Convergence, sampling, and fit

All models were fit with 4 chains (4,000 iterations; 1,000 warmup). The
table reports the maximum $\hat{R}$, minimum effective sample size
(ESS), the number of divergent transitions, and Bayesian $R^2$ (with 95%
CI) for each model. Convergence was good throughout: all
$\hat{R} \le 1.01$ and all ESS well above typical thresholds.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-diag
#| echo: false
D |>
  mutate(Model = recode(model, !!!mod_lab),
         `Bayes R² [95% CI]` = sprintf("%.2f [%.2f, %.2f]", BayesR2, BayesR2_low, BayesR2_high)) |>
  select(Model, `max R̂` = max_Rhat, `min ESS` = min_ESS,
         `divergences` = n_divergent, `Bayes R² [95% CI]`) |>
  kable(caption = "Table S1. Convergence and fit diagnostics for all models. Bayes R² is the Bayesian R² (proportion of variance explained) with its 95% credible interval [@gelman2019rsquared].")
```

A small number of divergent transitions occurred in two of the smaller
adult models (`study1_bf_adult`, `study3B_adult`). These did not affect
the conclusions; they can be eliminated by refitting with
`control = list(adapt_delta = 0.99)`.

## Posterior predictive checks

For each model we compared the observed distribution of responses to
draws from the posterior predictive distribution (`pp_check`, type =
"dens_overlay"). Predicted and observed distributions align well across
models.

```{r}
#| label: fig-ppc
#| echo: false
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-cap: "Posterior predictive checks for all models (pp_check, type = \"dens_overlay\"). In each panel the dark line is the observed distribution and the light lines are 100 draws from the posterior predictive distribution; for the binary outcome the density is bimodal (mass near 0 = fact and 1 = emotion), with peak heights reflecting the proportion choosing each. Observed and predicted align well across models."
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(diag_dir, "pp_all.png"))
```

# Full posterior estimates

For each study we report the posterior median, 95% credible interval,
percentage of the posterior in the ROPE, and probability of direction
for every model term (child models on the probit scale, with age
mean-centered so condition terms are evaluated at the mean age).

```{r}
#| label: tbl-s1
#| echo: false
post_table(c("study1_bf_child","study1_bf_adult",
             "study1_food_child","study1_food_adult")) |>
  kable(caption = "Table S2. Study 1 — full posterior estimates.")
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-s1cell
#| echo: false
post_table(c("study1_cellmeans_child","study1_cellmeans_adult")) |>
  kable(caption = "Table S3. Study 1 food-sharing cell-means model (reference = happy/candy).")
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-s2
#| echo: false
post_table(c("study2_child","study2_adult")) |>
  kable(caption = "Table S4. Study 2 (friend vs. best friend) — full posterior estimates.")
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-s3a
#| echo: false
post_table(c("study3A_child","study3A_adult")) |>
  kable(caption = "Table S5. Study 3A — full posterior estimates.")
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-s3b
#| echo: false
post_table(c("study3B_child","study3B_adult")) |>
  kable(caption = "Table S6. Study 3B — full posterior estimates.")
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-s4a
#| echo: false
post_table(c("study4A_child","study4A_adult")) |>
  kable(caption = "Table S7. Study 4A (child → mom vs. friend's mom) — full posterior estimates.")
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-s4b
#| echo: false
post_table(c("study4B_child","study4B_adult")) |>
  kable(caption = "Table S8. Study 4B (mom / friend's mom → child) — full posterior estimates.")
```

# One-sided binomial tests (all cells)

```{r}
#| label: tbl-binom
#| echo: false
B |>
  mutate(Study = study,
         Population = population,
         Cell = recode(cell,
           better_friend = "Better friend", ice_cream = "Ice cream", candy = "Candy",
           mom = "Mom", friendmom = "Friend's mom", friend = "Friend",
           bestfriend = "Best friend", create = "Create", deepen = "Deepen"),
         Emotion = tools::toTitleCase(emotion),
         `% emotion` = pct_emo,
         Direction = direction) |>
  select(Study, Population, Cell, Emotion, `% emotion`, Direction, BF10, BF01) |>
  arrange(Study, Population, Cell, Emotion) |>
  kable(caption = "Table S9. One-sided Bayesian binomial tests vs chance, in the pre-registered direction. BF10 favors the hypothesized direction; BF01 favors the null.")
```

# Social-closeness (circles) measure

In addition to the binary forced-choice items, in Study 1
(pre-registration Part 1) children and adults rated how close each
character was to the protagonist on a six-step overlapping-circles scale
(the Inclusion of Other in the Self measure; @aron1992inclusion). In the
raw data the most-overlapping ("closest") option was coded 1 and the
fully-separate ("least close") option 6; we reverse-scored the ratings
(7 − rating) so that higher = closer in all analyses below. This was a
pre-registered dependent variable for Study 1; we report it here because
it was not included in the main text due to children seemingly
insensitive to the measure in either pilot or the full sample.

```{r}
#| label: fig-circles-scale
#| echo: false
#| out-width: "40%"
#| fig-cap: "The six-step social-closeness (circles) scale shown to children (the actual stimulus from the pre-registration), adapted from the Inclusion of Other in the Self measure [@aron1992inclusion]. Children chose the option whose two circles best represented how close two characters were, ranging from fully separate (least close) to almost fully overlapping (closest). In the raw data these extremes were coded 6 and 1, respectively; analyses below reverse-score the ratings so that higher = closer."
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(fig_dir, "circles_measure.png"))
```

**Scale comprehension.** Two checks indicate children used the scale
appropriately. First, on the three warm-up items they placed different
relationships sensibly --- rating strangers as least close, a parent and
child as closest, and friends in between. Second, and more directly
relevant to the subtler classmate contrast, children's circle ratings
tracked their own forced choices: the classmate they selected as the
better friend (and as the ice-cream partner) received the closer circle
rating. The circles thus reflect the same closeness judgement as the
binary items. (The warm-ups, however, only calibrate gross relationship
distinctions; they do not include a fine, same-tier contrast such as
friend vs. best friend, so the graded results below are best read as
suggestive support for the binary findings.)

Following the pre-registration, we analyzed the (reverse-scored) ratings
with a Bayesian linear mixed-effects model,
`closeness ~ target * condition + (1 | ID)`, where *target* is the
classmate who was told the emotion vs. the fact and *condition* is the
happy vs. sad vignette. Descriptively, in the sad condition the
emotion-classmate was rated closer than the fact-classmate, whereas in
the happy condition the two were equivalent (Table S10) --- the same
pattern as the "better friend" choice in the main text.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-circ-means
#| echo: false
CM |>
  mutate(Population = recode(population, child = "Children", adult = "Adults"),
         Condition = tools::toTitleCase(emotion),
         `Character told` = recode(target, emo = "Emotion", fact = "Fact"),
         `Mean closeness` = sprintf("%.2f", mean),
         SD = sprintf("%.2f", sd), N = n) |>
  select(Population, Condition, `Character told`, `Mean closeness`, SD, N) |>
  arrange(desc(Population), Condition, desc(`Character told`)) |>
  kable(caption = "Table S10. Mean social-closeness (circles) ratings (reverse-scored, higher = closer) by population, condition, and which character was told the emotion vs. the fact (children N = 57, adults N = 47).")
```

In the model, the target × condition interaction was in the predicted
direction --- the emotion-classmate advantage was larger in the sad
condition (`r circ("b_targetemo:emotionsad")`) --- although its 95%
credible interval included zero; the two targets did not differ in the
happy condition (`r circ("b_targetemo")`). Thus the graded measure
points the same way as the binary "better friend" judgement, more
weakly. Full estimates are in Table S11 and the means are plotted in
@fig-circles.

For adults, who completed the same circle scale, we could recover the
emotion- vs. fact-target mapping because the adult study used a single
fixed survey version: each character's name was perfectly confounded
with the role it played, which we confirmed against adults'
better-friend choices and free-text justifications. Coded this way,
adults rated the emotion-character as markedly closer than the
fact-character --- a credible main effect of target
(`r circ("b_targetemo", "study1_circles_adult")`) --- in both the happy
and sad conditions; the target × condition interaction was not credible
(`r circ("b_targetemo:emotionsad", "study1_circles_adult")`). On the
graded scale, then, adults showed the same emotion-disclosure →
closeness inference as in their forced-choice judgements, and more
clearly than children did.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-circ-est
#| echo: false
CE |>
  mutate(Population = recode(model, study1_circles_child = "Children",
                             study1_circles_adult = "Adults"),
         Term = gsub("^b_", "", term),
         `95% CI` = sprintf("[%.2f, %.2f]", CI_low, CI_high)) |>
  select(Population, Term, Median = median, `95% CI`, `% in ROPE` = pct_in_ROPE, pd) |>
  kable(caption = "Table S11. Social-closeness (circles) model: posterior estimates (linear model on the reverse-scored 1–6 scale, higher = closer; reference = fact-target, happy condition).")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-circles
#| echo: false
#| out-width: "95%"
#| fig-cap: "Mean social-closeness (circles) ratings (reverse-scored; higher = closer) for the character who was told the emotion vs. the fact, by condition, for children and adults. Error bars = ±1 SE. Consistent with the binary 'better friend' choice, the emotion-character is rated closer (children: in the sad condition; adults: in both conditions)."
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(fig_dir, "circles_study1.png"))
```

In short, the graded circles measure corroborates the binary results.
For children, the emotion-character was rated closer (most clearly in
the sad condition), mirroring their "better friend" choices, though the
effect was suggestive rather than credible (its 95% credible interval
included zero) --- plausibly because the warm-ups did not calibrate
fine, same-tier distinctions. For adults, the emotion-character was
rated credibly closer, converging with the forced-choice results.

## Developmental change

We refit the (reverse-scored) closeness model with mean-centred
continuous age folded in,
`closeness ~ target * emotion * age + (1 | ID)`. There was no credible
developmental change: the main effect of age was null
(`r cage("b_age_c")`), as were the age × target interaction
(`r cage("b_targetemo:age_c")`) and the age × condition interaction
(`r cage("b_emotionsad:age_c")`). The three-way target × condition × age
interaction trended positive (`r cage("b_targetemo:emotionsad:age_c")`):
descriptively, the tendency to rate the emotion-classmate as closer
*specifically in the sad condition* strengthened with age
(@fig-circles-age), but its 95% credible interval included zero, so we
interpret it cautiously. This direction nonetheless mirrors the
developmental pattern in Study 2, where the sad-emotion preference for a
best friend also grew with age.

```{r}
#| label: fig-circles-age
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Study 1 circles, developmental. Model-predicted closeness (reverse-scored; higher = closer) across age for the classmate told the emotion vs. the fact, by condition (children). Bands = 95% credible intervals; points = individual ratings (jittered)."
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(fig_dir, "age_circles.png"))
```

# Gender effects

As an exploratory, non-pre-registered check, we tested whether the
child's gender moderated the results (gender was reported by the
caregiver). For each study we refit the child probit model adding the
child's gender and its interaction with emotion
(`chose_emotion ~ emotion * gender + (1 | ID)`), collapsing across the
relationship/measure factors so the test is uniform across studies.
Models compare boys to girls (the reference); the single child whose
caregiver reported a non-binary gender (Set B/C) was dropped because a
one-child group cannot be estimated. As elsewhere, we treat a term as
credible when its 95% credible interval excludes zero.

```{r}
#| label: gender-summary
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
gx   <- dplyr::filter(SX, term %in% c("b_genderM", "b_emotionsad:genderM"))
cred <- dplyr::filter(gx, CI_low > 0 | CI_high < 0)
if (nrow(cred) == 0) {
  cat(sprintf("Across the seven studies, **none** of the %d gender-related terms (a gender main effect and a gender × emotion interaction per study) had a 95%% credible interval excluding zero. We therefore found no evidence that boys and girls differed in their judgements.", nrow(gx)))
} else {
  labs <- gsub("_gender$", "", cred$model)
  cat(sprintf("Across the seven studies, %d of the %d gender-related terms had a 95%% credible interval excluding zero (%s); the remainder were null. Given the number of terms tested, we interpret any isolated effect cautiously.",
              nrow(cred), nrow(gx), paste(labs, collapse = ", ")))
}
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-gender
#| echo: false
gender_lab <- c(study1_bf_gender = "Study 1 — better friend", study1_food_gender = "Study 1 — food sharing",
             study2_gender = "Study 2", study3A_gender = "Study 3A", study3B_gender = "Study 3B",
             study4A_gender = "Study 4A", study4B_gender = "Study 4B")
SX |>
  filter(term %in% c("b_genderM", "b_emotionsad:genderM")) |>
  mutate(Study = recode(model, !!!gender_lab),
         Term = recode(term, b_genderM = "Boys (vs. girls)",
                       `b_emotionsad:genderM` = "Boys × sad"),
         `95% CI` = sprintf("[%.2f, %.2f]", CI_low, CI_high)) |>
  select(Study, Term, Median = median, `95% CI`, `% in ROPE` = pct_in_ROPE, pd) |>
  kable(caption = "Table S12. Exploratory gender effects: posterior estimates for the child's gender and its interaction with emotion, by study (probit scale; reference = girls, happy condition).")
```

# References

::: {#refs}
:::
